Nicole: Great. So my suggestion to you. I've been talking about this book so much in the last couple of days. I don't know why it's come back up. I think because I gave it to Linda to read. It's called Never Split the Difference and it's written by an FBI hostage negotiator. And it has some of the best tips for managing difficult conversations. And one of the tips that they have, there's.

Nicole: There's two that I use all of the time. One is speaking to the skepticism and the other one is using an extreme anchor. So I think both of these could be useful in these conversations that you're having with these potential or these particular clients. Speaking to the skepticism is calling out what it is that they would be fearful of. So in your case, if you've got fear, it's likely because, you know they are very price sensitive. And so when we sit down to discuss the variations, which I would suggest that you do, rather than just email them over so you can take that time with them to help them understand it. I would be speaking to that skepticism. Now I know this is very uncomfortable.

Nicole: I know this is not going to be what you want to hear, which is also a bit of an extreme anchor. I'll come back and explain that in a moment and then tell them what it is. So the idea of this speaking to the skepticism is we're calling it out, we're acknowledging it, which distills it a little bit, but it also gets them braced and ready for whatever's going to come next. And generally where they go in their mind is ten times worse than what you're actually going to say anyway. And so if we frame it like that, we open them up to be more receptive to the conversation we're about to have. And if you're prepared to show up and sit and have those conversations with them, like you said earlier, as you explain it out, you have the opportunity for it to make more sense to them. Because ultimately at the end of the day we can go back and build it to plan. At the end of the day they're making a change.

Nicole: You're gracious enough to accept that change. This is the cost associated. Let me explain it to you now. The issue I have with Queensland building contracts, it might be the same in every state. This might be one of those ones that's the same is the margin you can make on those variations is actually quite small. 20% is very little. So you absolutely must be putting that 20% on builders margin. It is written in the contract, it is clear for them to see.

Nicole: And I would also then think of other ways in which you could be marking up some of those variations to ensure that your office time, the paperwork time, the administration, dealing with the trades time, all of that is covered as well.
Dylan: Yeah. So we generally have two free variations and after that it's got a set rate starting at two hours. Admin?

Nicole: Yep. Perfect. Love that. Well done. Okay. The other one is the extreme anchor thing. So the extreme anchor thing works really, really well when we're trying to do any kind of business negotiation where there's financials involved. So for example, if I were to go, let's say I wanted.

Nicole: I'm trying to think of who I was talking to about this recently. One of our clients is looking at purchasing a property and they have a pretty good idea of what the property is worth. They've done some work with a buyer's agent to Figure out what it's worth. So the number is here. And basically what we've, with a lot of negotiation, talked them into doing is throwing out their extreme anchor, which is like down here. Now, we didn't want to make it in this particular case, too insulting, but we did want to give an anchor. It's the bottom dollar we're prepared to pay. Knowing that in the middle, there's a price that everybody's comfortable with.

Nicole: So if you're on the other side of the fence, your extreme anchor might be this really high price. Knowing that that's well over the value, but that's what you want out of it, that would be ideal. Also, knowing again, that there is this middle part where we all probably come to settle on. So I think that there you could also use something very similar to that when they first ask for the variations. So the conversation could potentially go, look, I really don't know what this is going to price up at, but I am thinking it could be in the vicinity of extreme anchor much, much higher. So that when you come in with your price and it's less than that, they actually already feel more comfortable. Yeah, it's a bit of psychology. So I think that.

Nicole: Well, first of all, I think you should all go and read the book or listening to the audiobook if you get the chance. It's a cracker. Also talks about body language and matching of pace and tone and all those things that are really helpful when you're in a negotiation. In your case, Brendan, I think those two corners, core messages could be really useful for putting you in a more favorable light because at the end of the day, you're doing them a favor.

Dylan: So I think the. The one with the kitchen and the laundry and walk in pantry, she's taken the laundry out, but then she's changed the floor plan and changed the kitchen layout. But her initial thought is that she's going to save money because she's moved the removed the laundry. But in fact, she's added stuff to the walk in pantry and it's actually increased the price, as I said, about 12, 13.

Nicole: It's crazy. I know some of these things don't make sense when we don't know. And that's the mentality that you're dealing with. Unfortunately, you're dealing with someone who doesn't know. So they make up a story in their head, which is why setting that extreme anchor is really important and having those early conversations so that when you then go in to deliver the variation, it's different to what they Thought so they're more like, oh, thank God, that's much less than I thought. I feel more comfortable now. I guess the part of this in variations that we don't often talk about, it's like another new mini sales process every time we have a variation and it's up to us to set the expectations for the consumer. And in the case like this, hello, handsome.

Nicole: We would do that by setting an extreme anchor. So they're feeling then far more confident when you come in under that anchor. And if you come in really close to it, then they're prepared. I don't. I don't necessarily not enjoy that bit at all.

But if I am by myself, whether I've got staff or not, that's usually when everything is working well. And so there was probably a period of time maybe two years ago that that was the case. But then also pre. Covered. There was probably a couple of periods there where I was, you know, that's pre TIB. I was doing like maybe 90 to 100 hours a week thinking that I was pretty smart.

The clients have changed again, except there's less of them because they've all got their landscaping done and they don't see the point in moving house because there still isn't the value there, whatever. So there's less work around as well.

Nicole: I think the issue is the consistency and the cons. I think the. That's the issue because it's still motivated by motivation, which is the hardest thing in the world. You can't motivate yourself to do something you don't want to do. Can you race gaily? And so I think that that's the place for discipline. But discipline is easy for some of us and really freaking hard for others of us. Yes, Reece, I agree. So I feel as though, I know this isn't available to you right now.

Nicole: It's a little bit like race. Not available to him right now. I think some of these areas where discipline is hard for us as business owners, we need to outsource it. I don't like making cold calls. I had to do them for myself until I got the business back into a position where I could pay somebody to do them for me. Now somebody does them for me and lo and behold, my calendar is all booked again. It's like my marketing. I had to do it myself until I got the business to a position where I could pay somebody to write all the scripts and stuff for me.

Nicole: Lo and behold, it's working better now than it has for quite some time. But I had to do it myself until we got to that point. For me, the motivation to be disciplined came from knowing that when I reached this target, I could financially afford to pay somebody else to do the Stuff I didn't like for me, I can't pay someone to film for me. I'd like to because I hate putting my face on camera. But people need to see who I am. So it's just something I've got to do whether I like it or not. Therefore, having someone to hold me accountable to that. I've paid somebody now and every month I.

Nicole: On the first Monday of the month, they're waiting for my videos to be sent through. And if they're not sent through, then I've wasted my money for that person for the month. And I don't like wasting money. So I think that the hard part for you and Reece, you're in a similar spot with this, is that right now we need to find a motivation to push you to discipline. So for some of us, it's money. We don't want to not have money. For others of us, it's pride. It's what somebody else thinks of us.

Nicole: For some of us, it might be, oh, some of us are just born with discipline. Some of us are just able to do discipline all of the time. And then some of us, it's fear. There'll be a fear that will motivate us into holding onto the discipline. The thing for you, Brains and Reece, I'm probably talking to both of you, or I'm talking to all of you. I think one of the best things you could do is figure out what, what it is that would move you towards where you need to be. Is it fear? Is it pride? Is it money? What is it? Because when we figure that out and we figure out, then that gives us the ability to figure out a strategy to put that in place so that we can then find the discipline to do the thing. So for me, it's very much money.

Nicole: I've had to get let go of the, the pride thing and the, the fear thing a long time ago because I've got to put my face on social media, so I've got to be judged. I've got to get the feedback I don't want to hear. I've got to go through that process because otherwise I can't have the business that I want. So now for me, it's money. If I don't do this by this time and I don't have these clients by this time, I put myself in a hole and I don't. I can't do that. I don't like the lack of money thing. So when we figure out what it is, it's just coming up with a strategy.

Nicole: Around that to help us with the discipline and always having someone to help hold you accountable to that. And it's no silver bullet and it's not going to work 100% of the time, but it'll be the closest thing to get you to that consistency of discipline that we're looking for. And once we get to that point and things are more comfortable then we can start to outsource it. We get somebody else to help us with the marketing, we get somebody else to do the cold outreach. We get somebody else to go and do print up a bunch of flyers and do a run for us. Like there are plenty of amazing people and AI resources that can do many of that, many of those tasks for us. We just have to work ourselves into a position where it's affordable. Thank you Runes Appreciate it and Reese appreciate it.

Nicole: Dylan Undercutting. Let's talk about undercutting because it's going to happen. If it has never happened to you, it's going to happen to you. People like to undercut. They think. Actually I spoke about it yesterday and today on our daily voxer brief talking about people feeling the fear and so they undercut, they underprice, they discount their prices, no problem. Brad Just so they can win the job. But essentially all of us can go broke at home.

Nicole: We don't have to be working for other people to put ourselves in a position that's uncomfortable financially. The power for all of us is in education. So Dylan, your education is two builders and there is. We often think we can't educate a builder because they often think I'm going to talk about myself and myself only. We think we're superior beings and we know all the things. And what would you know as a subcontractor?
Dylan: Right?

Nicole: That's the image everybody's got. It's not true. We still need education as well. But the education in your case is about the difference between you and the guy that's undercutting. And so it's about things like your guarantee, it's about how many quotes you do. It's about the way you approach your work, the way you help the builder. When you educate your builder around those areas, the undercutting becomes less of a problem. And yes, that won't stop them maybe from trialing someone who's less expensive than you.

Nicole: But you know as well as I do there'll be no after sale service. There'll be no support in any way if something goes wrong and they won't be coming back to do any touch ups or fix ups. Because the builders, you know, one of the laborers has knocked a scratch out of the wall as they've tried to carry the ladder through. But that's something you could possibly factor into your pricing so that there is that support for the builder as well. Once they get through, they have a go. The other guy's shit as they are. You and I both know what your trade is like. Then they're wanting to come back because you don't cause problems, you solve problems.

Nicole: So I think very much for any of you, regardless of what position you're in in the market. I think the key here is in education. We want to educate our clients or our potential clients to understand the difference between us and those that undercut the very best blog post that ever. There was a couple. But one of the very best blog posts that ever ranked on my website was a quote comparison blog post teaching people how to compare quotes. Now that doesn't work for you, Dylan. When you take it to a builder, yours would have to be around your quality, your inclusions, your after sales service, your defects, periods, all of those kinds of things. But for everybody else dealing with consumer, you've got the opportunity to do that education, help them understand the difference between your pricing and somebody else's.

Nicole: It's all inclusive, for example. Also, insurance is a huge thing. People underestimate how important insurance is. If I've got somebody coming in my home to do a repaint, for example, and they're not insured, and they're not over my priceless, I don't own one, but some priceless vase thing, my house insurance doesn't cover it. And if they don't have insurance, it's not going to get replaced. Or what happens if one of their people are dodgy and they steal stuff while they're there? It's not going to get covered by my home insurance. So you've got opportunities to educate in those ways. What about licensing? Why is licensing important? Educate your clients as to why they should be looking for a licensed tradie.

Nicole: Teach them how to go and check the licenses so they can see that's the stuff that's important to your consumer and helps separate you from the competition. You can do plenty of those kind of visual things as well, you know, unlicensed trading, licensed trading, where you show the differences. Like how many times have you seen, you know, a shitty apprentice come through and check out the wrong side of the door for the door handle? Or you see all these stupid mistakes that you can point out because they're not licensed, they don't know. They haven't done their Mastercraft. So I think they're the opportunities available to all of us. They should form part of our marketing for all of us all of the time. We just do it differently for a builder, Dylan.
